turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Responding to Feedback or a Performance Review

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Hi. I got feedback from my supervisor, and I’m not sure what to do.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That’s okay. Feedback can feel uncomfortable. Let’s go through it together. Was the feedback given in a meeting or in writing?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Email. Long email.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Alright. When you read it, what feeling did you have first?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Stress. Because I don’t know if it’s bad.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That’s understandable. Was the feedback mostly positive, mostly critical, or mixed?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Mixed. Some praise, some comments.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Let’s separate those. What’s one positive thing they mentioned?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: They said I’m reliable and careful.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That’s strong feedback. Reliability is very valued. Now, what’s one comment that confused you?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: They said I need to be more proactive. I don’t know how much is enough.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That’s a very good question. In your role, do you usually wait for instructions before acting?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Yes. Because if I do wrong, I’m scared.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That makes sense. “Being proactive” usually doesn’t mean taking big risks. It can mean small things, like asking questions earlier or offering help. Does that sound more manageable?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Yes, but… if I ask too many questions, is that annoying?

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Another excellent question. Usually, asking thoughtful questions is seen as engagement, not annoyance. It depends on timing and tone.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Okay. Another thing… should I reply to the email quickly, or wait?

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: It’s generally good to reply within a day or two. You don’t need a long reply, just a professional one.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: What if my English is not perfect in reply?

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That’s okay. Clear and polite is more important than perfect grammar. You can keep it simple.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Can I say I will try, even if I’m not sure how?

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Yes, and you can also ask for clarification. For example, you can say you’d like examples of being proactive in your role.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: That feels safer.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Good. Did the feedback mention any future review or follow-up?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Yes. Review again in three months.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That’s helpful. It gives you time to apply the feedback. You might even keep small notes about what you try, so you can show progress later.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Notes is good idea. Otherwise I forget.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Exactly. Before we finish, is there anything about the feedback that still feels uncomfortable?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: They didn’t say I’m bad. But I still feel like I did something wrong.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That feeling is very common. Mixed feedback often feels heavier than it is. Try to see it as guidance, not judgement.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Okay. I’ll try to think like that.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Let me summarise. You received mixed feedback, including praise for reliability and a suggestion to be more proactive. You’ll reply professionally, acknowledge the feedback, ask for clarification if needed, and track small improvements. Does that sound right?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Yes. That sounds right.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Excellent. You’re handling this thoughtfully.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Thank you.
